Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd have these bursts of intense studying when anxiety would hit me hard, usually right before something was due. I tried to set up regular times, but with 16 online courses and everything else going on, I'd end up cramming materials whenever I could physically sit down and focus. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often spend time practicing or working through problems offline rather than following a structured schedule on the LMS.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how scared I was about falling behind. Some weeks I'd barely log in, and then suddenly I'd have a marathon session where I'd watch hours of videos and download everything I could. Deadlines definitely drove my engagement more than any internal schedule I tried to create. The anxiety would build up and then I'd have these crisis moments where I'd do everything at once.
Q3: Usually fear, if I'm being honest. I'd get this wave of panic thinking about what I might be missing or what deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or new assignments posted that I hadn't seen. The LMS became this thing I both avoided and obsessively checked depending on my anxiety levels that day.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became a comfortable routine for me. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in more regularly, but then something would throw me off—work, personal stuff, or just feeling overwhelmed—and the habit would break. I think my attendance being so low reflects that I couldn't sustain consistent engagement even when I wanted to.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could download them and watch at my own pace, pausing to take notes or rewind when I didn't understand something. I also found the practical exercises useful since I'm a kinesthetic learner—I need to actually do things to understand them. Discussion boards were okay for seeing how others approached problems, though I didn't post much myself.
Q6: I basically ignored the peer interaction stuff entirely. I know I have zero peer interactions logged, and that's because reaching out to others felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up. Some of the supplementary readings I'd skip too, especially when I was in crisis mode and just trying to get the essential assignments done. If it wasn't directly tied to my grade, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate, tangible results from doing it. Like if I could practice something and then apply it to an assignment right away, that felt valuable. I also appreciated anything that helped reduce my anxiety about the course—clear instructions, practice quizzes that showed me where I stood. Vague or overly theoretical content felt like a waste when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required work got done because the fear of failing pushed me through, but optional stuff? That required mental energy I just didn't have. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping them, which only added to my anxiety. It's like, I knew they might help, but I was in survival mode.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded almost everything so I could study offline without the pressure of being logged in. I'd print materials, make physical flashcards, and practice problems in notebooks. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my computer, especially when I needed to move around or work through things with my hands. The LMS only saw me download stuff, not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a video once and then watch it three or four times on my tablet while doing other things around the house. I'd take my printed notes to work and review during breaks. I had study sessions with downloaded PDFs that lasted hours but the LMS just showed one quick download. My actual engagement was way higher than my login frequency suggests.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my main strategy. I'd do a big download session, grabbing everything I might need, and then work through it all offline over the following days. It helped with my anxiety to have materials accessible without needing to log in and see all the things I was behind on. The LMS interface itself stressed me out sometimes, so working offline was a relief.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. My anxiety would spike so high that I'd freeze instead of engaging. Sometimes competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars or just life stuff would pile up, and the course would slip. Those moments when I felt scared and overwhelmed were when my attendance dropped the most. The mindfulness exercises helped a bit, but bouncing back was hard with my low resilience.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or confused about what was expected, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because opening it felt like opening a box of stress. Competing responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go days without logging in, then have to play catch-up. The workload across all my courses was intense, and confusion about priorities made me freeze up rather than dive in.
Q14: Oh yes, plenty of times. I'd log in, click around, maybe start a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too anxious to absorb anything. There were sessions where I'd have the LMS open for an hour but couldn't tell you a single thing I learned. My body was there but my brain wasn't.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt too big or unclear. The fear of doing something wrong sometimes paralyzed me more than motivated me. I'd know an assignment was important but convince myself I needed to understand everything perfectly before starting, which meant I'd delay until the last possible moment. It's a terrible cycle that my anxiety feeds into.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving sometimes. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before due dates—downloading materials, watching videos at 2x speed, frantically completing assignments. My LMS usage probably shows these spikes clearly. Without deadlines, I'd drift, but with them looming, the fear would kick in and I'd suddenly become very active.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. My anxiety made it hard to receive feedback without catastrophizing. I'd sometimes delay checking my grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with 16 online courses happening simultaneously. When modules were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could manage better. But when things were vague or self-paced, I'd fall behind because I didn't have that external pressure pushing me. I needed more structure than was sometimes provided to keep my anxiety from taking over.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could study in ways that worked for my kinesthetic learning style. But the lack of clear progress indicators sometimes made it harder—I couldn't always tell if I was on track, which fed my anxiety. Also, discussion requirements felt forced and added pressure without feeling genuinely useful to my learning.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking through things, maybe rewatching videos without really processing them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, too stressed to actually complete the work but too scared to step away from the course entirely.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My final grade was a Merit despite my low attendance and engagement scores because I was doing substantial work offline. I'd download everything, study intensively on my own, and then submit assignments that reflected real understanding. The LMS couldn't see the hours I spent with printed materials or practicing skills away from my computer.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, what they download, how long videos play. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is actually learning or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture offline study, emotional state, or whether someone understood the material. For someone like me who studies mostly offline, the logs tell maybe 30% of the story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and working through problems because I want to learn, not just because I'm scared of failing. Real engagement for me is hands-on practice, making connections between concepts, and feeling like the learning matters. It's not about time logged in—it's about mental investment.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much fear drove my engagement, or how the mindfulness interventions actually helped me calm down enough to study. It wouldn't capture the physical notebooks full of practice problems, the materials I reviewed during lunch breaks at work, or the genuine understanding I developed despite my chaotic approach. It also can't show how hard I fought against my anxiety to complete this course with a Merit despite everything working against me.
